title: Forced-alignment adapter (audio↔text → SMIL/WebVTT/JSON)
context: Forced alignment — matching audio to its source text to produce precise word/sentence timing — is the core technical step that turns a narration into a text-aligned read-along. The cold-start narrate-001 task performs this manually; adapter-001 wraps the chosen forced aligner behind a neutral interface so that future batch runs can be automated. The adapter must be evaluatable against the alignment reference set from narrate-001 so accuracy is reproducible over time. It must also flag low-confidence segments for human review rather than silently shipping drift. Depends on data-001 (record schema) and narrate-001 (reference set).
objective: Implement the forced-alignment adapter in adapters/align/ that wraps the chosen aligner (e.g. aeneas, Montreal Forced Aligner, or WhisperX) behind a neutral interface and emits EPUB 3 Media Overlays (SMIL), WebVTT, and a JSON sidecar at configurable granularity.
acceptanceCriteria: Wraps the chosen forced aligner behind a neutral TypeScript interface in adapters/align/; the aligner implementation is swappable without changing callers.
Emits EPUB 3 Media Overlays (SMIL), WebVTT, and a JSON sidecar for each input audio+text pair at configurable granularity (word-level and/or sentence-level).
Measures onset/offset error against the alignment reference set from narrate-001 and reports median onset error per run.
Flags low-confidence segments for human alignment review (does not silently pass them); includes a per-segment confidence score or flag in the JSON output.
Emits a run report: per-file median onset error, count and fraction of segments flagged for human review, and aligner version.
Passes pnpm build and pnpm test (unit tests covering at least: correct SMIL/WebVTT/JSON structure, low-confidence flagging, error handling for mismatched audio/text lengths); pnpm lint clean.
Licensed MIT; DCO sign-off on all commits.
output: A merged PR adding adapters/align/ with the forced-alignment adapter implementation, unit tests, and documentation covering the neutral interface, output formats, confidence-flag semantics, and how to run against the reference set.
